title: Albóndigas en Salsa
subtitle: Spanish meatballs in tomato sauce
excerpt: Albóndigas en salsa are Spanish meatballs of pork and beef simmered in a rich tomato and sherry sauce. The meatballs are seasoned with garlic, parsley, and a little cumin, browned in olive oil, and finished in the sauce. Serve with crusty bread or over rice.
sourceNotes: Albóndigas are found across Spain as a tapas dish and as a home-cooked main course. The combination of pork and beef mince with a tomato and sherry sauce is the common form. Sherry vinegar or dry sherry lends a distinctive Spanish character to the sauce.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool and freeze the meatballs in their sauce. Reheat gently on the hob.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Keeps in the fridge for 3 days.

content.text: Albóndigas are Spanish meatballs of mixed pork and beef, seasoned with garlic, parsley, and cumin, simmered in a tomato and dry sherry sauce. They are smaller and more delicate than Italian-style meatballs and the sauce is sharper and more wine-forward. A Spanish tapa served by the dozen, or a main course for four.

Mix the pork mince, beef mince, garlic, breadcrumbs, beaten egg, parsley, and {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin in a large bowl. Season well with salt and pepper. Shape into balls about 3 cm across; you should get 20 to 24 meatballs. Dust lightly with plain flour.
Heat {{olive-oil}} in a large frying pan over medium-high heat. Brown the meatballs in batches, turning carefully, for about 5 minutes until golden on all sides. Transfer to a plate.

Reduce the heat to medium. Cook the onion in the same pan for 5 minutes. Add the garlic and {{paprika-smoked}} of smoked paprika and stir for 1 minute. Pour in {{sherry-dry}} of dry sherry and let it bubble for 2 minutes. Add the tinned tomatoes and season. Simmer for 5 minutes.
Return the meatballs to the sauce. Cover and simmer gently for 15 minutes until cooked through. Serve scattered with a little chopped parsley.

items.symptom: The meatballs break apart when browned
items.cause: Mixture too wet, or moved too early
items.fix: Leave the meatballs undisturbed in the pan for at least 2 minutes before turning. They form a crust and release from the pan naturally when ready.

items.symptom: The sauce tastes too sharply of sherry
items.fix: Let the sherry bubble for 3 to 4 minutes until most of the harsh alcohol has cooked off before adding the tomatoes.

content.text: Albóndigas appear on tapas menus across Spain and are one of the dishes most often made at home for children and adults alike. As a tapa the meatballs are small and served a dozen at a time in a clay dish. As a main course four to six per person in the sauce is the right serving. Bread alongside is the only accompaniment required.
